text: The surgeon identifies and ligates the inferior mesenteric vein at its origin, marking the initial step of the procedure.
The surgeon initiates dissection adjacent to the trite ligament, where the identification of the inferior mesenteric vein is visually facilitated.
The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric vein and applies clips at its origin, securing the vessel.
The surgeon detaches the mesocolon from the pancreatic edge and divides the torus fascia from the gyrotus plane, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the colorectal procedure.
The surgeon opens the peritoneal layer towards the pelvis to visually identify the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric artery and proceeds to dissect the sigmoidal artery, clipping it at its origin while preserving the origin of the mesenteric artery.
The surgeon dissects the parietal peritoneum along the lateral margin of the colon, starting from the proximal sigmoid colon and extending towards the splenic flexure.
The surgeon performs a posterior dissection of the mesorectum, following Heald's technique, and progresses the dissection downward into the pelvis.
The surgeon completes the isolation of the posterior part of the mesorectum and proceeds with lateral dissection, separating the rectal wings from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum anteriorly, one centimeter from the Douglas reflection, facilitating the complete removal of the Douglas pouch.
The surgeon continues the dissection in front of the denim VA's fascia, carefully separating the fascia from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to separate the distal rectum, completing the dissection in this area.

Following the transection, the surgeon performs an indocyanine green fluorescence angiography to visually assess the vascularization of the colorectal region. This angiography is repeated after completing the colorectal anastomosis.
